title: Transactions & Connection Pooling
learningObjectives: Explain **Transactions & Connection Pooling** with production trade-offs, SLO thinking, and failure modes
Implement small **Java** snippets that anchor interview whiteboard discussions
Connect **observability** and **capacity** signals to real operational decisions
Identify common **pitfalls** that cause incidents or cost surprises at scale
Compare alternatives using **constraints** (latency, cost, team skills, compliance)
Present answers with **structure**: problem, options, decision, verification

sections.title: Why Transactions & Connection Pooling matters
sections.content: Pools and transactions decide whether your service **fails** gracefully or **collapses** the database. Interviewers expect **HikariCP** knobs and **isolation** honesty.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Transactions & Connection Pooling
sections.content:
### Day 69 — **Transactions** & **pooling**

### 1. Pool sizing
**Little’s law** intuition; **CPU** vs **wait** dominated workloads.

### 2. Timeouts
**Connection**, **socket**, **statement** layers.

### 3. Spring @Transactional
**Proxy** semantics, **rollback** rules, **self-invocation** pitfalls.

### 4. JDBC basics
**AutoCommit**, **batch** updates.

### 5. Observability
**Active**, **idle**, **pending** threads; **wait** metrics.

### 6. Story
**Pool** **exhaustion** during sale—raise **pool** only after proving DB **headroom** and fixing **slow** queries.

sections.title: Basic — Pool vocabulary
sections.output:
pool: reuse connections; cap total concurrency to DB
transaction: atomic unit of work with ACID expectations

sections.title: Intermediate — Pool size toy
Advanced — Timeout ordering reminder
App to pool to DB
sections.plantuml:
@startuml
title Day 69 — Pooling
participant App
participant Pool
database DB
App -> Pool : getConnection
Pool -> DB : physical conn cap
@enduml

sections.title: Exercise — Transactions & Connection Pooling (Day 69 assignment)
sections.problem:
Print **exactly** two lines:
1. Implemented: Transactions & Connection Pooling
2. Focus: HikariCP tuning for high concurrency.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What does **HikariCP** optimize for?
conceptual.answer: Fast handoff, minimal overhead, and predictable pool metrics under load. Contrast **happy** **path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split** **retries**, **half-open** **probes**, **degraded** **read** modes, and **idempotency** keys that prevent **duplicate** **writes**. Mention **verification**: **`curl -v`** through **gateway**, **`kubectl logs`**, **APM** **waterfall** **spans**, and **dashboards** that slice **p99** by **downstream** **service** **name**.

followUps.question: Production follow-up: which **dashboards** or **metrics** expose this for **transactions and pooling**?
followUps.answer: Start with **client** **outbound** **histograms** and **resilience4j** **circuitbreaker** **state** **gauges**, then open **distributed** **traces** filtered by **service** **name** and **route**. Fast handoff, minimal overhead, and predictable pool metrics under load. Interviewers reward naming **metrics** (`http_client_requests_seconds`, `resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state`), **trace** correlation IDs, and **rollback** criteria rather than hand-wavy resilience claims. Tie answers to **Spring** reality: **Feign** + **LoadBalancer**, **WebClient** + **`ExchangeFilterFunction`**, **Resilience4j** registries, and **Micrometer** tags that make **on-call** searches fast.

followUps.question: How do you **roll out** a policy change here without a **retry** **storm**?
followUps.answer: Use **feature** **flags**, **canary** **deployments**, **tight** **retry** **budgets** on **non-idempotent** calls, and **verify** with **load** tests that **match** **production** **fan-out**. Fast handoff, minimal overhead, and predictable pool metrics under load. Mention **verification**: **`curl -v`** through **gateway**, **`kubectl logs`**, **APM** **waterfall** **spans**, and **dashboards** that slice **p99** by **downstream** **service** **name**. Senior answers separate **symptom** from **root** **cause**: **502** at **edge** can be **empty** **Endpoints**, **stale** **pool**, or **upstream** **panic**—each needs different **first** **commands**.

conceptual.question: **maxPoolSize** vs **database** **connection** **limits**?
conceptual.answer: Pool must stay below DB max connections across all instances combined. Reference **security**: **mTLS** **service** identity, **OAuth2** **scopes**, **audit** logs at **edge**, and why **secrets** never belong in **Feign** **URL** query strings. Contrast **happy** **path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split** **retries**, **half-open** **probes**, **degraded** **read** modes, and **idempotency** keys that prevent **duplicate** **writes**.

Pool must stay below DB max connections across all instances combined. Call out **ownership**: **platform** **SRE** for **mesh**/**certs**, **service** teams for **timeout** policy, **SRE** for **SLO** **burn** **alerts**—and how those teams **coordinate** during **sev-1** calls. sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Knob | Recall |
|---|---|
| maxPoolSize | Per service budget |
| connectionTimeout | Wait for free conn |
| leakDetectionThreshold | Find leaks |
| validationTimeout | Health check cost |
| autoCommit | false for tx |
| isolation | Business choice |
| rollbackFor | checked exceptions |
| batch | reduce round trips |
| socket timeout | network bound |
| statement timeout | query bound |
